title: Recurrent-event hazard model with shared frailty
summary: Pit stops are recurrent time-to-event decisions, not isolated IID rows.
rationale: Pit stops are recurrent time-to-event decisions, not isolated IID rows. Survival literature emphasizes discrete hazards with time-varying covariates and shared latent effects for repeated events; here that latent effect is likely team/driver/race strategy aggressiveness, especially for later stints.
implementation_hint: Reframe each row as a discrete next-lap hazard example, add baseline hazard bins over `TyreLife` or `laps_since_last_stop`, and include cross-fitted frailty proxies such as prior stint-length residuals by `Driver`, `Race`, `Year`, and `Compound`. Blend this hazard model with the current best GBDT rather than replacing it.
expected_effect: Sharper probabilities around 2nd/3rd stop windows and fewer race-specific calibration misses than a plain binary classifier.
risk: High overlap with prior hazard ideas if implemented too similarly; validation must purge within-race chronology so frailty features do not peek forward.
